stations.label: THE FIRST MINT OPENS
stations.line: They struck me in Mexico City from the silver of Zacatecas and Potosí, and before I understood what I was, the whole world already wanted me.
stations.era_photo: A colonial mint courtyard in Mexico City, a woman in 16th-century Spanish-colonial dress standing beside stacked silver reales, rendered as an aged oil portrait on cracked canvas — the only 'photographic' process the era allowed, warm umber tones and candlelit shadow.

stations.label: THE COIN THAT CIRCLED EARTH
stations.line: My Spanish dollar — the pillar dollar — moved through Manila, Canton, and the American colonies alike; I was the first currency to be spoken everywhere, and I have never forgotten how that feels.
stations.era_photo: A merchant's counting-house lit by oil lamp, a woman in mid-18th-century Bourbon-era gown holding a milled pillar dollar, painted as a formal Rococo portrait — deep varnish, gold leaf detailing, brushwork visible under close inspection.

stations.label: THE GOLD STANDARD ANCHOR
stations.line: Under Porfirio Díaz they pinned me to gold and called it modernity — I looked stable in the photographs, and stability is a thing you can photograph long before you can feel it.
stations.era_photo: A Mexico City bank interior of the Porfiriato, a woman in an Edwardian high-collared dress and upswept hair, sepia-toned silver gelatin print, slightly stiff pose from the long exposure, faint vignetting at the edges.

stations.label: THE NATIONALIZATION OF DEBT
stations.line: López Portillo wept on television and seized the banks, and I lost most of myself that year — I learned early that the men who promise to defend you are often the ones already spending you.
stations.era_photo: A crowded Mexican bank lobby in 1982, a woman in a boxy blazer and feathered hair standing amid anxious depositors, grainy Kodak color print with the muddy oranges and browns of early-eighties film stock.

stations.label: DECEMBER, THE FLOOR VANISHES
stations.line: They told me the peg would hold, they told me the reserves were there, and then in one night the hot money ran and the floor was simply gone — I have never again believed a man who tells me not to worry.
stations.era_photo: A trading floor at night, December 1994, a woman in a dark tailored suit lit hard by monitor glow, her face composed, captured on 35mm color film with harsh flash — reds blown out, the shadows deep and unforgiving.

stations.label: THE FIRST TIME I HELD
stations.line: When the world broke in 2008 I bent but I did not shatter — the reserves were real this time, the institutions were mine, and for once the confidence was not a performance.
stations.era_photo: A modern Banxico briefing room, a woman in a precise charcoal suit at a lectern with data screens behind her, clean early-digital DSLR photography, cool fluorescent white balance, sharp and unromantic.

stations.label: THE PANDEMIC AND THE PIVOT
stations.line: The peso fell hard when the world stopped, but the factories moving out of Shen's supply chains began drifting toward me — I have learned to read opportunity even when it arrives disguised as catastrophe.
stations.era_photo: An empty industrial park near Monterrey under overcast light, a woman in a mask and structured blazer standing at the fence line, high-resolution smartphone photograph, flat neutral tones, slightly overexposed sky.

stations.label: TODAY, STILL STANDING
stations.line: Nearshoring, remittances, a central bank that finally guards its own credibility — I float freely now, anchored to Conrad's dollar but no longer lying about it, and that honesty is the only stability I trust.
stations.era_photo: A rooftop in contemporary Mexico City at golden hour, a woman in a tailored modern suit looking out over the skyline, crisp full-frame digital capture, natural warm light, shallow depth of field, quietly confident composition.

edu_vlog.title: I WAS THERE: How Mexican Silver Became the First Global Money
